SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper frames sarcasm interpretation as monolingual MT and evaluates phrase-based Moses, an RNN encoder-decoder and the proposed SIGN method, using human adequacy/sentiment evaluations and correlation analyses (sections describing BLEU, PINC, human measures and Pearson correlations). The experimental setup appears reasonable (baselines, human evaluation, qualitative analysis, use of SentiWordNet and embeddings), but the excerpt lacks detailed quantitative results and formal significance testing for the main adequacy/sentiment claims, so minor methodological details are missing though they do report some statistical testing for correlation significance.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper explicitly introduces a novel task — sarcasm interpretation — and a new dataset of 3000 sarcastic tweets with five human interpretations, noting that "to the best of our knowledge, generation of sarcasm interpretations has not been previously attempted." It also proposes SIGN, an MT-based algorithm that specifically targets sentiment words, which is a new perspective beyond standard MT/paraphrasing approaches.

CLARITY.justification: The paper follows a standard organization (problem definition, methods, experiments, discussion) and states contributions early (dataset, MT framing, SIGN). However, the provided excerpt contains repeated sentences and some formatting/duplication issues that reduce readability, and technical notation/details (e.g., full model/hyperparameter descriptions) are sparse in the excerpt.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The work includes a new dataset, multiple baselines (Moses, RNN), a targeted method (SIGN), human evaluations (fluency, adequacy, sentiment), qualitative analysis and discussion of failure cases (e.g., world-knowledge examples). While these aspects provide solid depth, the excerpt lacks full ablation/sensitivity studies and complete reproducibility details (numerical results, full hyperparameters) which are left as minor gaps.

IMPACT.justification: Sarcasm interpretation addresses an important problem for sentiment analysis and opinion mining (paper cites challenges for these applications) and the dataset plus task formulation open a clear new research direction. The contribution is likely to be influential within NLP for pragmatic language understanding even if not transformative across all of ML.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (sarcasm interpretation, dataset, MT/paraphrasing methods) squarely fits the scope of top-tier NLP venues, uses established MT and evaluation methodologies (Moses, BLEU, METEOR, PINC, RNN encoder-decoder), and is aimed at the appropriate research community.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares the proposed SIGN against reasonable baselines (phrase-based Moses, RNN encoder-decoder) and an oracle upper bound, discusses why n-gram automatic measures fail for this task, and analyzes qualitative differences. However, the excerpt does not show exhaustive quantitative comparisons or whether other recent paraphrasing/style-transfer baselines were considered, so some relevant baselines may be missing.
